quotes.verbatim_quote:
Sweet Georgia Brown starts on the VI7. |t wouldn’t seem like such a
surprising way to start if it was a minor 7 chord (meaning it would be
the relative minor). However, the vi minor is converted into a VI domi-
nant.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains the defining harmonic feature of the tune — the non-diatonic conversion of vi to VI7 — which shapes improvisation and reharmonization choices throughout.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
just
know that it is entirely normal and acceptable to turn minor chords in
the diatonic series into dominant 7“ chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the permissive rule that underpins the entire harmonic analysis of this song and recurs across multiple jazz standards in the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
bars 1-13 are just a long cycle of dominant 7* chords resolving to the |
chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the macro structural summary of the A-section that a player must internalize before attempting to improvise over it.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Don't let this section throw you either. Understand we are just dealing
with a series of chromatically descending dominant 7“ chords that are
targeting the VI7 chord. From there the VI7 logically concludes to a ii-
V-| to the parent tonic.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names and demystifies the chromatic turnaround device so students can recognize and navigate it in other standards.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can simplify your understanding of this section by noting that it
starts on the I7 and is targeting the VI7. You can think of bars 29-32
as a “backdoor” |7-VI7-ii-V-I.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Offers a reductive label ('backdoor') that makes the chromatic section memorable and transferable to other tunes.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The Mixolydian scale is the 5th mode of the major scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the foundational modal definition students need before applying the scale as an improvisational tool over dominant 7 chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The easiest way to think of a Mixolydian scale is a major scale with a
b7. Or you can think of it as starting and ending a major scale on its
5* tone.

Formula: 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives two practical shortcuts plus the formula for memorizing and constructing the Mixolydian scale on any root.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The
best way to think about the use of scales is “pitch collections.” They
aren’t notes that are meant to be played in order, nor are they to be
used solely to improvise. They are simply pitch collections which map
out the note choices you can use to make melodies over a particular
chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reframes scales from linear finger exercises to harmonic resource pools — a conceptual shift central to the method's improvisational philosophy.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Note that each bar starts on a different chord tone of the given chord.
The first and last lines begin with quarter note chord tones moving in
ascending order: R-3'*-5*-b7. The second line descends in this order:
R-b7-5°%-3°¢.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the exact structural logic of the Mixolydian exercise so students can replicate and extend it to other chord changes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
| always suggest taking things through all 12 keys. It’s a powerful
practice that not only helps you internalize musical information, but it
also enables you to become stronger in keys you may be weaker at.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates the method's core practice rule for lick internalization, applicable beyond this chapter to every improvisation lesson in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Starting in the second half of bar 24 we begin to transition into the
relative minor key. What's the relative minor to Ab major? F minor.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Marks the structural pivot to the relative minor, giving students the roadmap they need to switch scale and chord-tone choices mid-chorus.
